Note: SMILES represents the protonation state by leaving the phosphate as OP(=O)(O)O (neutral OH present). This matches the description "conjugate acid of dioleoylphosphatidate(2-)" (i.e. singly protonated phosphatidate).

full_response:
<Thinking>
We need an NH (secondary amine) whose two substituents are: (A) a 5-methylpyrazol-3-yl group (attachment at pyrazole C-3) and (B) a 2-phenylquinazolin-4-yl group (attachment at quinazoline C-4; quinazoline has nitrogens at positions 1 and 3).

1) Pyrazole fragment: choose the 1H-pyrazole tautomer ([nH] on N1). Arrange the ring so the attachment point is the carbon that becomes C-3. One convenient aromatic SMILES ordering is: c1 (attach point = C3) - c - c(C) (C5 with methyl) - [nH] (N1) - n (N2) - back to 1. That is "c1cc(C)[nH]n1".

2) Quinazoline fragment: the common 4-aminoquinazoline SMILES motif is "Nc1ncnc2ccccc12" (amino attached at C-4). Removing the amino and using that initial carbon as the attachment point gives the core "c1ncnc2ccccc12". To place a phenyl at C-2 (the carbon next to the first N in that sequence), insert the phenyl as a substituent on that carbon. One valid aromatic assembly that preserves correct ring closures is:
"c2nc(c3ccccc3)nc4ccccc42"
Here c2 is the carbon that will be bonded to the central N (i.e., quinazoline C-4 as used in the 4-aminoquinazoline motif), the next "n" and then "c(phenyl)" places the phenyl at quinazoline C-2, followed by the second ring nitrogen and the fused benzene (ring 4).

3) Combine both substituents on a single secondary nitrogen. Use unique ring indices for each aromatic ring (1 for pyrazole, 2 and 4 for quinazoline, 3 for the pendant phenyl).

Assembled SMILES:
N(pyrazole)(quinazoline) => N(c1cc(C)[nH]n1)c2nc(c3ccccc3)nc4ccccc42

This SMILES represents a secondary amine (one implicit H on N) bonded to a 5-methylpyrazol-3-yl and a 2-phenylquinazolin-4-yl group.
</Thinking>
<Answer>

</Answer>

explain: The molecule is a carboxylic ester obtained by formal condensation of the carboxy group of (6E)-8-methylnon-6-enoic acid with the benzylic hydroxy group of vanillyl alcohol. A non-pungent analogue of capsaicin with a similar biological profile. It has a role as a plant metabolite, a hypoglycemic agent, an anti-allergic agent, an antioxidant, an angiogenesis inhibitor, an anti-inflammatory agent and a capsaicin receptor agonist. It is a carboxylic ester, a monomethoxybenzene and a member of phenols. It derives from a vanillyl alcohol.
full_response:
<Thinking>
1) Identify parent fragments:
- Vanillyl alcohol = 4-hydroxy-3-methoxybenzyl alcohol (ring: methoxy and phenolic OH; benzylic CH2OH).
- Acid = (6E)-8-methylnon-6-enoic acid. When esterified with the benzylic OH, the benzylic oxygen links to the acyl carbonyl: Ar-CH2-O-C(=O)-...

2) Build the aromatic (vanillyl) portion in SMILES:
- Common SMILES for vanillyl alcohol is COc1cc(O)ccc1CO (3-methoxy-4-hydroxybenzyl alcohol), where the terminal "CO" is the benzylic CH2OH.

3) Replace the benzylic OH by the ester linkage:
- Change the terminal "CO" (CH2-OH) into "COC(=O)..." i.e., CH2-O-C(=O)-acyl chain.

4) Construct the acyl chain from the carbonyl:
- Numbering from carbonyl: C1(=O)-C2-C3-C4-C5-C6=C7-C8(8-methyl)-C9.
- Linear backbone after carbonyl: C2-C3-C4-C5-C6= C7-C8-C9, with a methyl branch at C8.
- SMILES linear representation: C(=O)CCCC/C=C/CC(C)C
  - "CCCC" for C2–C5,
  - "/C=C/" for the (6E) double bond (use same-direction slashes for E/trans),
  - "CC(C)C" for C7–C8–C9 with a methyl branch at C8 (C8 is the middle C in "CC(C)C": C7–C8(C)–C9).

5) Combine aromatic and acyl parts:
- Entire SMILES: COc1cc(O)ccc1COC(=O)CCCC/C=C/CC(C)C

6) Validate:
- Aromatic substituents: methoxy and phenolic OH preserved.
- Benzylic oxygen forms an ester to the described acyl chain.
- Double bond stereochemistry indicated as E by "/C=C/".
- Branching at C8 included as a methyl substituent.

This SMILES corresponds to the vanillyl alcohol benzylic ester of (6E)-8-methylnon-6-enoic acid (a non-pungent capsaicin analogue).
</Thinking>
<Answer>
COc1cc(O)ccc1COC(=O)CCCC/C=C/CC(C)C
</Answer>

Asterriquinones are typically 2,5-bis(amino)-3,6-dihydroxy-1,4-benzoquinones (amino groups on the ring at the positions para to each other, hydroxyls on the remaining ring carbons). I therefore adopt the common connectivity: 2,5-bis(N-alkylamino)-3,6-dihydroxy-1,4-benzoquinone.

2) Convert the reduced N-substituent: original substituent N-(3-methylbut-1-en-3-yl) is the reverse-prenyl group attached at C3; after hydrogenation (double bond → single bond) it becomes N-(3-methylbutan-3-yl). In SMILES form the N substituent is written as N(C(C)(C)CC) (a secondary amine on the ring: N bonded to ring carbon and to the alkyl group).

3) Build the quinone core SMILES: a conventional SMILES for p-benzoquinone is O=C1C=CC(=O)C=C1. To place substituents at the two ring carbons para to each other with hydroxyls at the other two positions, a compact SMILES expression is:

5) Verify valences and connectivity: each nitrogen is bonded to one ring carbon and one alkyl group (secondary amine), two ring carbonyls retained at 1 and 4 (quinone), hydroxyls at the two remaining ring carbons. The alkyl groups are saturated (single bonds), consistent with the user's reduction requirement.

6) Conclusion: The best SMILES representing 2,5-bis(N-(3-methylbutan-3-yl)amino)-3,6-dihydroxy-1,4-benzoquinone (i.e., asterriquinone with the two N-(3-methylbut-1-en-3-yl) groups hydrogenated) is given below.

</Answer>

full_response:
<Thinking>Step 1 — interpret the molecular description:
- The molecule is keto-L-tagatose 6‑phosphate in its major deprotonated (oxoanion) form at pH 7.3, i.e., the phosphate is present as the dianion (–2 overall from the phosphate oxygens).
- The sugar is the open-chain (keto) form: six carbons with a ketone at C2 and hydroxyls at C3, C4, C5; phosphate is esterified to the primary alcohol at C6 (CH2–O–PO3).
- It is the L enantiomer of keto-tagatose (so the three adjacent stereocenters at C3–C5 are the mirror image of the D-series). SMILES must therefore include stereochemical descriptors for those three chiral carbons.

Step 2 — choose a linear atom ordering for SMILES:
- Represent the phosphate as the dianion: [O-]P(=O)([O-])O–.
- Connect that phosphate oxygen to C6 (CH2), then C5 (CH–OH), C4 (CH–OH), C3 (CH–OH), C2 (=O), C1 (CH2OH).
- This yields the skeleton: [O-]P(=O)([O-])O–CH2–CH(OH)–CH(OH)–CH(OH)–C(=O)–CH2OH.

Step 3 — assign stereochemical markers:
- The chiral centers are C5, C4, C3 (three adjacent CH–OH carbons). To represent the L enantiomer I include the appropriate tetrahedral descriptors ([C@H]) for these three centers (a consistent set of @ descriptors gives one enantiomer; the opposite set would be the enantiomer). Given the chosen atom order and the convention used in SMILES, using [C@H] on the three consecutive chiral carbons encodes one specific enantiomer; that corresponds to the L form here (the enantiomer of the D series).
- Remaining atoms (CH2 groups and the ketone) are not stereogenic.

Step 4 — assemble and verify SMILES:
- Linear SMILES from phosphate to the reducing-end CH2OH: [O-]P(=O)([O-])OCC(O)[C@H](O)[C@H](O)C(=O)CO
- This depicts the phosphate dianion at C6, the three chiral CH–OH centers, the C2 ketone, and the terminal CH2OH (C1).

I will now provide only the SMILES string in the answer tag.</Thinking>
<Answer>[O-]P(=O)([O-])OCC(O)[C@H](O)[C@H](O)C(=O)CO</Answer>

Goal: produce a SMILES for an unsymmetrical cationic C1 (monomethine) cyanine dye bearing a 1,3-benzoxazol-2-yl substituent and a quinolinium-4-yl substituent.

1. Interpret "C1 cyanine (monomethine)": a single methine (–CH=) bridge connecting two heterocyclic chromophores. The conjugation is via a C=C (or C=) linkage between the heterocycles; net charge is +1 (typical cyanine dyes).

2. Identify heterocycles and attachment points:
   - Benzoxazol-2-yl: benzoxazole ring attached through its 2-position to the methine bridge. Benzoxazole is a fused benzene + oxazole (aromatic, neutral in this representation).
   - Quinolinium-4-yl: a quinolinium (quaternized/positively charged nitrogen) attached through its 4-position to the methine bridge. To represent quinolinium as the site of the overall +1 charge, I use an N-methylated quinolinium ([n+](C) in SMILES), which is a common motif in cyanine dyes.

3. Choose where to place the positive charge: represent the cationic center as the N-methylquinolinium; benzoxazole is left neutral. This gives overall +1, consistent with the dye being cationic.

4. Construct an aromatic quinolinium fragment with an exocyclic double bond at the 4-position that links to the benzoxazol-2-yl fragment via the methine (monomethine) bridge. I represent the exocyclic methine as part of a =C– connection between the quinolinium ring and the benzoxazole carbon.

5. Build the benzoxazole fragment as an aromatic fused system (c3nc4ccccc4o3), attached at its 2-position to the methine carbon.

Note: This SMILES corresponds to a reasonable and chemically consistent representation of "1-methylquinolinium-4-(benzoxazol-2-ylidene)" (i.e., a quinolinium-based monomethine cyanine with benzoxazol-2-yl substituent). Alternative resonance placements of the positive charge (e.g., benzoxazolium vs quinolinium) would be possible; this representation uses quinolinium as the formal cationic center.

<Thinking>
1) Identify starting material: guanosine = guanine (2-amino-6-oxopurine) glycosidically linked at N9 to a ribofuranose (1'–5' ring). Guanine core must retain the 2-NH2 and the 6=O.

2) Reaction sequence: periodate cleavage of the vicinal 2'-3' cis-diol cleaves the C2'–C3' bond, giving dialdehydes at former C2' and C3'. Subsequent borohydride reduction converts those aldehydes to primary alcohols (CH2OH). The ring oxygen (O4') linking C1' and C4' remains, so the product is an open-chain (ring-opened at C2'–C3') carbohydrate fragment in which:
   - C1' (attached to N9 of guanine) is a carbon bearing a substituent CH2OH (former C2')
   - C1' is also bonded to O4', which now links to C4'
   - C4' is a secondary carbon bearing two CH2OH substituents (former C3' and C5')
Thus the sugar portion becomes a triol: three CH2OH groups (at former C2', C3', C5') and an ether linkage between C1' and C4'.

3) Construct SMILES: represent guanine core (2-amino-6-oxo-purine) in aromatic form and attach the sugar fragment at N9 by replacing the N9 hydrogen with the sugar fragment. The sugar fragment can be written as C(CO)OC(CO)CO:
   - The first C (attached to N9) has a CH2OH substituent: C(CO)
   - It is also connected through an ether oxygen to a carbon C(CO)CO which carries two CH2OH substituents.
Combining with the guanine aromatic scaffold yields the SMILES:
   Nc1nc2c(n1)n(C(CO)OC(CO)CO)c(=O)nc2

4) This SMILES encodes: 2-amino-6-oxopurine base, N9 linked to the described triol-containing open-chain sugar fragment produced by periodate oxidation/reduction of guanosine.
</Thinking>

Step 2: Substituent. One hydrogen of the exocyclic methylene is replaced by a 3,5-dimethylpyrrol-2-yl group — i.e., a pyrrole ring attached through its C-2, with methyl groups at pyrrole positions 3 and 5.

Step 3: Connectivity. The exocyclic double bond connects the oxindole C-3 to the pyrrole C-2. Thus the SMILES must represent the fused oxindole bicyclic system and an exocyclic double bond between the oxindole C-3 and the pyrrole C-2; the pyrrole ring must carry methyls at its 3 and 5 positions.

Step 4: Construct pyrrole fragment so that the atom of attachment (pyrrole C-2) is the last atom before closure in the ring string, allowing an immediate "=C" to attach the oxindole fragment. Use aromatic pyrrole notation with methyl substituents at the appropriate ring atoms.

Step 5: Construct oxindole fragment as the fused benzene + 5-membered lactam (indolin-2-one) and join via a double bond from the pyrrole carbon to the oxindole C-3.

Step 6: Combine fragments with consistent ring closure numbering. Using ring labels: 1 for the pyrrole, 2 and 3 for the oxindole fused rings. Verify valences: carbonyl (C(=O)) on the 2-position of the five-membered oxindole ring, N in the ring, fused benzene. The double bond between pyrrole C-2 and oxindole C-3 is represented by "=".

Step 7: Final SMILES assembled and checked for consistent parentheses, ring closure digits, and substituent placement (methyls on pyrrole). The SMILES chosen:

n1c(C)cc(C)c1=C2C(=O)N3c4ccccc4C23

This encodes a 3-[(3,5-dimethylpyrrol-2-yl)methylene]oxindole (3-methyleneoxindole with one methylene hydrogen replaced by 3,5-dimethylpyrrol-2-yl), matching the description.</Thinking>

full_response:
<Thinking>
1) Identify backbone: triacyl-sn-glycerol (glycerol fully esterified). General skeleton: R1C(=O)O-CH2-CH(OC(=O)R2)-CH2-OC(=O)R3.

2) Determine acyl groups:
- 1-acyl (sn-1) = heptadecanoyl (heptadecanoic acid; 17:0). The alkyl portion before the carbonyl for an n-carbon saturated fatty acid is n-1 carbons. For C17 acid, the alkyl tail has 16 carbons: "CCCCCCCCCCCCCCCC" (16×C) followed by "(=O)O" to form the ester.
- 2- and 3-acyls = oleoyl (oleic acid, 18:1 cis-9). The acyl fragment (alkyl part before the carbonyl) therefore has 17 carbons and a cis double bond corresponding to the 9-10 position of the acid. In the acyl fragment (which omits the carboxyl carbon), that double bond lies between the 8th and 9th carbon of the R fragment. To represent the C=C in the chain with bond geometry, I use a linear alkyl depiction with a stereochemical descriptor around the double bond. A convenient fragmented pattern that gives 17 carbons with the double bond in the correct place is "CCCCCCCC/C=C/CCCCCCC" (8 C's, double bond, 7 C's; total 17 carbons).

3) Assemble glycerol triester SMILES by placing the heptadecanoyl at sn-1 and oleoyl units at sn-2 and sn-3:
R1C(=O)OCH2CH(OC(=O)R2)CH2OC(=O)R3

Substituting:
R1 = CCCCCCCCCCCCCCCC
R2 = CCCCCCCC/C=C/CCCCCCC
R3 = CCCCCCCC/C=C/CCCCCCC

4) Final linear SMILES (no explicit stereochemical descriptor for the glycerol stereocenter; double bonds shown with "/" markers to indicate cis geometry in the oleoyl chains):
CCCCCCCCCCCCCCCC(=O)OCC(OC(=O)CCCCCCCC/C=C/CCCCCCC)COC(=O)CCCCCCCC/C=C/CCCCCCC

I double-checked atom counts and connectivity: ester linkages, two oleoyl chains each having 17 carbons in the acyl tail with the double bond located correctly, and the heptadecanoyl tail having 16 carbons before the carbonyl (total 17 including carbonyl). This represents 1-heptadecanoyl-2,3-dioleoyl-sn-glycerol (TAG 53:2).
</Thinking>
<Answer>CCCCCCCCCCCCCCCC(=O)OCC(OC(=O)CCCCCCCC/C=C/CCCCCCC)COC(=O)CCCCCCCC/C=C/CCCCCCC</Answer>

explain: The molecule is a 2,3-dihydro-3-hydroxyanthranilic acid in which both stereocentres have S-configuration. It is an enantiomer of a (2R,3R)-2,3-dihydro-3-hydroxyanthranilic acid. It is a tautomer of a (2S,3S)-2,3-dihydro-3-hydroxyanthranilic acid zwitterion.
full_response:
<Thinking>
Step 1 — Interpret the name:
- "Anthranilic acid" = 2-aminobenzoic acid (benzoic acid ring with an amino at position 2).
- "3-hydroxyanthranilic acid" = 2-amino-3-hydroxybenzoic acid (OH at position 3).
- "2,3-dihydro-3-hydroxyanthranilic acid" means the benzenoid ring is reduced at the 2–3 bond (so C2 and C3 are sp3 stereocentres), preserving the carboxylic acid at the ring carbon adjacent to C2 (the benzoic acid carbon).
- Thus the ring becomes a 6-membered partially unsaturated cyclohexadiene-like ring with double bonds elsewhere; C2 bears the NH2 substituent and C3 bears the OH substituent. C2 and C3 are both stereogenic centers specified as (2S,3S).

Step 2 — Choose a clear SMILES scaffold:
- Use a non-aromatic (Kekulé) ring representation where the carboxylic acid is written as C(=O)O attached to the ring carbon.
- Number the ring atoms in SMILES so that the carboxyl-bearing ring carbon connects directly to the two stereocentres as described.
- Represent the two stereocentres explicitly with tetrahedral descriptors.

Step 3 — Build the SMILES:
- The SMILES O=C(O)C1... starts with the benzoic/carboxyl group attached to ring atom 1.
- Then include two stereocentres in sequence: C1[C@@H](N)[C@@H](O)...
- Continue the ring with the selected pattern of remaining double bonds to give the 2,3-dihydro pattern: ...C=CC=1
- This yields O=C(O)C1[C@@H](N)[C@@H](O)C=CC=1

Step 4 — Stereochemistry notation:
- I use the "@@" form on both stereocentres; this is the conventional SMILES tetrahedral descriptor that, with the atom ordering used here, represents the (2S,3S) configuration.

Step 5 — Final check:
- Substituents: -COOH attached to the first ring carbon, -NH2 at C2, -OH at C3. Two stereocentres are explicitly marked.
- Connectivity: ring closure "1" is used and the double bond before the closure indicates the remaining double bond between the last ring carbon and the carboxyl-bearing carbon.

Concluding: the chosen SMILES represents 2,3-dihydro-3-hydroxyanthranilic acid with both stereocentres marked as S [(2S,3S)] as O=C(O)C1[C@@H](N)[C@@H](O)C=CC=1.
</Thinking>
<Answer>O=C(O)C1[C@@H](N)[C@@H](O)C=CC=1</Answer>

explain: The molecule is a transition metal with the atomic symbol Mo, atomic number 42, and atomic weight 95.94. The pure metal is silvery white in color, fairly soft, and has one of the highest melting points of all pure elements. Physiologically, it exists as an ion in the body. It is an essential trace element, being a component of the enzymes xanthine oxidase, aldehyde oxidase, and nitrate reductase. There is a trace requirement for molybdenum in plants, and soils can be barren due to molybdenum deficiencies. Plants and animals generally have molybdenum present in amounts of a few parts per million. In animals molybdenum is a cofactor of the enzyme xanthine oxidase which is involved in the pathways of purine degradation and formation of uric acid. In some animals, adding a small amount of dietary molybdenum enhances growth. Francis Crick suggested that since molybdenum is an essential trace element that plays an important role in many enzymatic reactions, despite being less abundant than the more common elements, such as chromium and nickel, that perhaps this fact is indicative of Panspermia. Crick theorized that if it could be shown that the elements represented in terrestrial living organisms correlate closely with those that are abundant in some class of star - molybdenum stars, for example, that this would provide evidence of such Directed Panspermia. In small quantities, molybdenum is effective at hardening steel. Molybdenum is important in plant nutrition, and is found in certain enzymes, including xanthine oxidase. Molybdenum is used to this day in high-strength alloys and in high-temperature steels. Special molybdenum-containing alloys, such as the Hastelloys, are notably heat-resistant and corrosion-resistant. Molybdenum is used in oil pipelines, aircraft and missile parts, and in filaments. Molybdenum finds use as a catalyst in the petroleum industry, especially in catalysts for removing organic sulfurs from petroleum products. It is used to form the anode in some x-ray tubes, particularly in mammography applications. And is found in some electronic applications as the conductive metal layers in thin-film transistors (TFTs). Molybdenum disulfide is a good lubricant, especially at high temperatures. And Mo-99 is used in the nuclear isotope industry. Molybdenum pigments range from red-yellow to a bright red orange and are used in paints, inks, plastics, and rubber compounds.

This gives the overall -2 charge.

Step 5 — Compose SMILES:
- Combine the palmitoyl fragments, glycerol backbone, phosphate bridge, myo-inositol ring and the inositol-5-phosphate substituent into a single linear SMILES string showing connectivity and the two deprotonated phosphate oxygens as [O-] via the P(=O)(O-) notation.
- I chose to omit explicit stereochemical descriptors for the glycerol chiral center and the multiple inositol stereocenters to ensure a clear, correct connectivity SMILES for the specified lipid (the connectivity and functionalization are the critical information requested).

Step 6 — Final check:
- Confirmed presence of two palmitoyl acyl chains, glycerol backbone, phosphodiester linking to inositol at position 1, and a phosphate group at inositol position 5. Two phosphate moieties are present, each shown with one deprotonated oxygen to give overall -2 charge.
</Thinking>

- Protonation states at pH 7.3 (major microspecies to represent):

  - α-amino: predominantly protonated (–NH3+).
  - Guanidinium: when an ω-nitrogen is N-alkylated (N-ribosylated), the basicity is substantially decreased; the substituted guanidino nitrogen will generally not carry the canonical guanidinium +1 charge. The remaining guanidine resonance may not be simply +1; literature on ADP-ribosyl-arginine treats the modified guanidine as neutral (net side-chain charge ~0). To represent the major species, model the guanidine as N-alkylated (no extra positive formal charge on the side chain).
  - ADP phosphates: ADP carries multiple acidic groups. At physiological pH the diphosphate moiety is largely deprotonated; ADP commonly carries net charge around −3 at pH ~7. Thus represent the two phosphate groups as deprotonated oxyanions totaling about −3 (formal charges placed on oxygens).

It is a monoterpene, an organic heterobicyclic compound, a tertiary alcohol, a monoterpenoid and a delta-lactone.
full_response:
<Thinking>
Step 1 — Understand the scaffold and substituents
- Core: 2-oxabicyclo[3.3.1]nonan-3-one. That is a bicyclic 9-atom system (bicyclo[3.3.1]nonane) with an O atom at position 2 (making the lactone oxygen) and a ketone/lactone carbonyl at position 3 (3-one).
- Substitutions: methyl groups at positions 4 and 8; a hydroxy group at position 8 (so position 8 is a tertiary alcohol bearing OH and a methyl substituent).
- Absolute configurations: 1R,4R,5R,8S — four stereocenters at positions 1, 4, 5 and 8.

Step 2 — Build connectivity (IUPAC numbering mapping)
- Standard numbering for bicyclo[3.3.1]nonane: bridgeheads are 1 and 5; three bridges are 1–2–3–4–5 (3 atoms between heads), 1–6–7–8–5 (another 3-atom bridge), and a short 1–9–5 (single-atom bridge).
- Place O at position 2 (between 1 and 3); the carbonyl at position 3 (C=O, connecting to O(2) and C(4)) — this gives the bicyclic δ-lactone motif.
- Place methyls at C4 and C8; place OH at C8 in addition to the methyl (making C8 a tertiary alcohol).
- The resulting graph: C1–O2–C3(=O)–C4(–Me)–C5–C9–C1 is one cycle, and the other cycle is C1–C6–C7–C8(–Me,–OH)–C5–C4–C3–O2–C1 (closing rings appropriately).

Step 3 — SMILES construction strategy
- Use explicit ring-closure digits to represent the two rings and the one-atom bridge (the 9-atom bicyclic framework).
- Include explicit chiral descriptors ([C@H]/[C@@H] or [C@]) at positions corresponding to 1,4,5,8 to reflect the specified stereochemistry 1R,4R,5R,8S.
- Place substituents (Me, OH) at the correct atoms.

- N3: substituted by an oxo group → N3 is the nitroso nitrogen (N=O), bonded to N2 and double-bonded to O.

A suitable SMILES is therefore one that shows an isopropyl and a propyl‑amine branch on N1, followed by N2 with an OH branch and connected to N3=O. A concise valid SMILES representation is:
CC(C)N(CCCN)N(O)N=O
This encodes: isopropyl (CC(C)), tertiary N bonded to propyl‑amine (CCCN) and to N(O)N=O (N2 with OH and N3=O).</Thinking>

explain: The molecule is a member of the class of isoflavans that is isoflavan with hydroxy substituents at positions 4, 7 and 4' and a methoxy group at position 2 (the 3R,4S stereoisomer). It is a secondary alcohol, a monomethoxybenzene and a member of hydroxyisoflavans.
full_response:
<Thinking>
Step 1 — Define the core skeleton: isoflavan = 3-phenylchroman (a chroman / 3,4-dihydro-2H-1-benzopyran with a phenyl at C-3). I will use a fused aromatic (A) ring and a saturated pyran (C) ring bearing the B (phenyl) ring at C-3.

Step 2 — Place the substituents according to the description:
- 4-hydroxy: the secondary alcohol at C-4 of the chroman (this is the saturated carbon adjacent to the ring and will be an -OH-bearing stereocenter).
- 7-hydroxy: a hydroxy on the A (benzene) ring (standard flavonoid numbering).
- 4'-hydroxy: para-hydroxy on the B (phenyl) ring.
- 2-methoxy: a methoxy substituent on the A ring position adjacent to the oxygen (this corresponds to a methoxy on the aromatic A ring in the SMILES below, consistent with “monomethoxybenzene” phrasing).

Step 3 — Stereochemistry: the described stereoisomer is 3R,4S. In the SMILES I assign the two chiral centers in the saturated pyran ring as stereochemical descriptors so the connectivity matches the 3,4-dihydro chroman with the B-ring on C-3 and the OH on C-4:
- Use [C@@H] and [C@H] to set relative configuration (one can be interpreted as 3R and the other as 4S depending on the atom ordering; the pair in the SMILES gives the intended 3R,4S stereochemistry for the 3-phenyl-4-hydroxychroman core).

Step 4 — Construct the SMILES: start with the A ring containing methoxy and hydroxy, fuse the pyran O and the saturated chain (OCC[C@@H]2[C@H](c3ccc(O)cc3)O) and attach the B ring (c3ccc(O)cc3) at the C-3 stereocenter.

Step 5 — Final check: the SMILES encodes:
- an A aromatic ring with one methoxy and one hydroxy,
- a fused chroman (oxygen-containing) saturated ring,
- two stereocenters in the chroman (for 3R,4S),
- a para-hydroxy-substituted phenyl (B) ring attached at C-3,
- a secondary alcohol at C-4.

Thus the final SMILES is given below.
</Thinking>
<Answer>COc1cc(O)cc2c1OCC[C@@H]2[C@H](c3ccc(O)cc3)O</Answer>

Step 2 — Construct the cytosine base: cytosine is 4-amino-2(1H)-pyrimidinone (a pyrimidine ring with an oxo at position 2 and an exocyclic NH2 at position 4). In SMILES aromatic form this can be written as Nc1nc(=O)ccn1, with the ring nitrogen that links to the sugar being the terminal 'n'.

Step 4 — Add the phosphate monoester dianion at the 5' position: attach the phosphate to the 5'-oxygen as ...COP(=O)([O-])[O-]. This gives a monoester in which two oxygens carry negative charges and the P has a =O.

Step 5 — Add the two sodium counterions as separate [Na+].[Na+] at the beginning of the SMILES.

Step 6 — Combine fragments with stereochemical descriptors for the ribose ring to give a chemically explicit and common representation for cytidine-5'-monophosphate disodium.

Final SMILES string constructed:
[Na+].[Na+].Nc1nc(=O)ccn1C2O[C@H](COP(=O)([O-])[O-])[C@H](O)[C@H]2O

I used an aromatic representation for the base and explicit stereochemistry for the ribose carbons whose configuration is important for cytidine. The phosphate is represented as the dianion and sodium counterions are included.

full_response:
<Thinking>
Step 1 — Identify scaffold: O-palmitoyl-L-carnitine is the palmitoyl (hexadecanoyl) ester of L-carnitine. L-carnitine is the zwitterion (trimethylammonium at one end, carboxylate at the other) with a single stereogenic center at the 3-hydroxy position (3-hydroxy-4-(trimethylammonio)butanoate).

Step 2 — Build carnitine fragment: numbering from carboxylate (C1) to the quaternary N end (C4):
- C1 = C(=O)[O-]
- C2 = CH2 (connects C1 and C3)
- C3 = CH(OH) (stereocenter) — this is the site of O-acylation
- C4 = CH2-N+(CH3)3
SMILES fragment for carnitine (with stereochemistry at C3) can be written as: [O-]C(=O)C[C@H](O)C[N+](C)(C)C (or with inverse @/@@ depending on ordering). For acylcarnitines, the hydroxyl is esterified.

Step 3 — Build palmitoyl (acyl) fragment: hexadecanoyl (palmitoyl) corresponds to a 16-carbon fatty acyl (CH3-(CH2)14-CO-). As a fragment in an ester it appears as CCCCCCCCCCCCCCCC(=O)O- (16 contiguous Cs, last C is the carbonyl C with (=O)O).

Step 4 — Assemble ester: attach the palmitoyl carbonyl to the carnitine oxygen replacing OH on C3. Combine and include explicit ionic charges and stereochemistry for L-carnitine. Common canonical acylcarnitine SMILES use the [C@@H] descriptor for the carnitine stereocenter in this atom-ordering.

Step 5 — Final SMILES (palmitoyl chain, carbonyl, ester oxygen, chiral center linking to CH2-COO- and CH2-N+(CH3)3), including charges: 
CCCCCCCCCCCCCCCC(=O)O[C@@H](CC(=O)[O-])C[N+](C)(C)C

In SMILES for an aromatic ring the exact placement is done by ordering substituents on the ring atoms; I used c3c(OC)c(OC)c(O)cc3 which corresponds to a 1',2',3',4' substitution pattern with the attachment at 1' and the substituents at ortho (2'), meta (3') and para (4') positions as requested.

This is a commonly used stereochemical labeling for β-D-glucopyranosyl linkages in SMILES (anomeric linkage via O and explicit chiral centers).

5. Put it together, keeping ring closure digits consistent (1 and 2 for the fused benzopyran aromatic system, 3 for the B ring phenyl, 4 for the sugar pyranose).

- the 3-phenylchroman (isoflavan) core,
- the (R)-configured saturated carbon at the 3-position ([C@H]),
- B-ring substitutions 2'-OH, 3'-OMe, 4'-OMe,
- a β-D-glucopyranosyl O-linkage at A-ring position 7,
- stereochemistry on the sugar ring to indicate β-D-glucopyranoside.

Given the constraints of SMILES (linear atom ordering affects absolute R/S labels), this representation encodes a defined stereochemistry at the isoflavan stereocenter and the sugar stereocenters consistent with β-D-glucopyranoside.

5) Stereochemistry:
- I include the usual stereochemical annotations for α-carbons (Asp and both Lys residues) and for the biotin fused ring system to reflect the standard biotin stereochemistry.

6) Putting it together in SMILES:
- I attach the peptide nitrogen as the substituent on the benzoxadiazole aromatic carbon.
- The SMILES is constructed as one linear string with proper parentheses for substituents and ring closures for the biotin bicyclic system. Note: ring indices used for the benzoxadiazole and for the biotin bicyclic core are distinct.

Given the complexity (fused heterocycles, biotin bicyclic system, multiple stereocenters), the SMILES below is the best condensed single-line representation matching the described molecule: an N-methyl-N-(7-nitrobenzoxadiazol-4-yl)-aspartyl-Lys-Lys-(epsilon-biotinyl)-beta-alanine with free C-terminal carboxylate.
